problem_summary: 2011 MCM/ICM Problem C (interdisciplinary): assess the feasibility and impact of widespread electric-vehicle adoption replacing petroleum cars — specifically how to make electric cars "catch on," how much fossil fuel is saved, the economic feasibility, and how to augment the power grid to meet the new electricity demand. Team #11422 split this into a micro problem (how the electric-car fraction E grows over 50 years given a government incentive/"seeding" program) and a macro problem (how to re-mix the power grid to meet the added demand while trading off cost against pollution), then meshed the two to recommend an optimal seeding proportion.
models_used: Car-fleet sizing model: cubic polynomial population fit (valid to ~2060) multiplied by a 0.002t per-capita growth factor to give total cars C(t) as a function of years-since-1950; split into E(t)=E·C(t) electric and G(t)=(1-E)·C(t) gas

Global Influence (adoption-diffusion) model: coupled ODEs for E'(t) and G'(t) driven by replacement of broken-down cars, where the probability an individual buys electric equals the current electric proportion E/(E+G); breakdown rates BDE=1/20 (electric lifetime ~20 yr) and BDG=1/8 (gas lifetime ~8 yr)
Euler's method to numerically integrate the coupled adoption ODEs from t=60 (year 2010) forward 50 years, with a government 'seeding' initial condition

2D Cellular Automata (Moore-neighborhood, 8-cell) model of localized peer-influence adoption, with per-cell stay/switch probabilities P(stay electric)=(1/8)(.1N+.1) and P(gas→electric)=(1/20)(.1N+.1) as a function of electric neighbors N

Snowball/global-local blending: reduced the ODE to a discrete logistic-type update (ΔE_p = (3/40)·E_p·(1-E_p)) and combined local L with global W via weighted average P=(k·L+W)/(1+k) using snowball constant k=4 (locality = 80% of behavior)

Pollution metric: a dimensionless weighted sum over pollutants {CO2, CO, NOx, SO2, particulates} of (j_G + E·j_E)/j_T (emitted plus electric-saved, normalized by total), with an optional per-pollutant 'badness' factor (set to 1 in all runs)

Energy cost model: per-source price assumed to rise quadratically with added production p(L)=i − 3iL/m + 12iL²/m², integrated to a total-cost cubic; calibrated by anchor points (2.5× cost at half-max, 10× cost at max production), summed over 5 sources
Scalarized multi-objective optimization: objective X = Cost + α·Pollution, with α encoding how much the government values environment vs. cost

Nelder-Mead simplex search (primary) and a genetic algorithm (cross-check) to minimize X over 5 power-source allocation variables, subject to box constraints (each source between current production and current·1.25) and a total-demand constraint
α-sweep / Pareto-style lookup: iterate Nelder-Mead across α to produce 'Cost vs α' and 'Power source vs α' curves, then read off allocations from a budget line (e.g. $200B → α≈37,500)
Fossil-fuel-saved closed form F(t) ≈ 19.68·E·C(t) gallons/year from mileage (12,200 mi/yr), 31 mpg, electric lifetime

Meshing model: feed seed proportion → micro model → E in 2060 → macro model → power-grid cost, then add seeding cost + grid cost to find the total-cost-minimizing seed (found 5–10%, or 1% at very high α)
key_techniques: Decomposition into micro (adoption dynamics) and macro (energy/economics) sub-models with an explicit 'meshing' section that chains the micro output (E in 2060) into the macro input
Using two independent adoption models (continuous ODE + discrete cellular automata) for the same phenomenon, then comparing them — CA showed local interactions slow diffusion vs. the ODE
Framing policy as a tunable parameter: the α weight turns a hard ethical question ('how much do you value the environment?') into a single dial, enabling budget-driven lookup tables
Calibrating an unknown supply/demand curve from sparse anchor points (current, half-max, max prices) into a closed-form quadratic price function, then integrating analytically for total cost
Normalizing heterogeneous pollutants by their total emissions so percent-reduction (controllability) is weighted rather than raw poundage — an explicit modeling choice with a worked rationale
Cross-validating the optimizer: running both a genetic algorithm and Nelder-Mead, confirming equivalent optima, then keeping Nelder-Mead for speed

Honest constraint engineering: encoding real-world limits (no plant removal → lower bound at current production; infeasibility of >25% capacity expansion → upper bound) directly as optimization constraints
Producing actionable, concrete deliverables: a specific recommendation (seed 5–10% of cars in 2011) plus exact QBTU additions per source (e.g. natural gas +2.75, coal +6.25, nuclear +2)
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper won because it is genuinely interdisciplinary and answers EVERY part of the prompt — adoption, fuel saved, economic feasibility, AND grid augmentation — instead of cherry-picking one sub-question. It pairs a behavioral diffusion model with a constrained optimization model and explicitly meshes them, demonstrating systems thinking rather than two disconnected exercises. The α-parameter is the standout idea: it converts an unanswerable values question into a tunable knob and produces a usable decision tool (budget → α → power-grid allocation), which reads as policy-relevant and sophisticated to judges. Methodologically it shows range and maturity — ODEs + Euler, cellular automata, a quadratic-cost economic model, and TWO optimizers cross-checked against each other — while staying numerically concrete (real EIA/BP energy data, specific pollutant tables, dollar figures, QBTU recommendations). Crucially it is intellectually honest: nearly every section has a candid Strengths & Weaknesses subsection, and assumptions are stated as replaceable-with-better-data, which signals scientific maturity. The result is a clean, end-to-end story from individual buying behavior to a national grid plan with a crisp, specific final recommendation.
section_highlights: Structure is the paper's quiet strength. It opens with Clarification of Problem and an explicit 3-step Plan of Attack that previews the micro→macro→mesh arc, so the reader always knows where they are. Assumptions are front-loaded as a bulleted list, each with a one-line justification AND an escape hatch ('can be replaced with reliable data when available'), which preempts judge objections. The body mirrors the plan exactly: Section 5 builds C(t); Section 6 (Micro) gives two adoption models each with its own Strengths & Weaknesses; Section 7 (Macro) walks cleanly through demand → pollution → cost → α → optimization → worked example; Section 8 (Meshing) is the payoff that connects them with multi-α cost tables; Section 9 delivers a one-paragraph concrete recommendation. The repeated per-model Strengths & Weaknesses subsections (6.1.1, 6.3, 7.10) are a model of self-critique. Worked 'Example Calculation' (the $200B budget → α≈37,500 graphical lookup) makes the abstract optimization tangible and usable. Equations are numbered and defined inline, and figures (adoption curves, CA grid, cost-vs-α, source-vs-α) are tied to specific claims.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The cubic population fit C(t) is self-admittedly only valid to ~2060 and will diverge afterward — a fragile foundation since several downstream results depend on it (the CA model is noted as a partial hedge because it has no population growth)

The adoption ODE's core assumption — P(buy electric) = current electric proportion — is asserted, sourced mainly to a documentary ('Who Killed the Electric Car?'), not calibrated to data; the snowball constant k=4 (80% locality) is essentially picked

Heavy reliance on convenience assumptions stated but not validated: prices rise quadratically with demand, energy-source mix is globally uniform = US 2009 mix, 100% fossil-to-electricity conversion efficiency, and a hard 25% cap on capacity expansion that directly bounds the optimizer
The α parameter, while clever, has an admittedly 'ambiguous/veiled' meaning — it is a relative knob with no physical units, so its real-world interpretation is left to the reader
Pollution 'badness' weights were all set to 1 (every pollutant equally bad), collapsing a key health/environment distinction the paper itself raises but does not resolve

Apparent data/typo errors in the meshing tables (e.g. some α=200,000 and α=500,000 rows are identical, and stray exponents like 10^11 vs 10^12 in Total Cost columns), and the per-car seeding cost line ('$41,000−$28,400=$12,600') is garbled in the text
Single electric-car spec (Li-ion Motors, 120 mi range, 0.44 kWh/mi) generalized to the entire future fleet; ignores hybrids by assumption and waves away range anxiety as affecting only 10% of users

Genetic algorithm was run but discarded for speed, so the global-optimum claim rests on Nelder-Mead, which the paper notes shows piecewise 'feasible pocket' behavior — i.e. possible local-minimum sensitivity
reusable_patterns: Micro→Macro→Mesh decomposition: model individual behavior, model the aggregate system separately, then explicitly chain one's output into the other's input and optimize over the linking variable (here: seed proportion)
Scalarized objective X = Cost + α·(other objective) with an α-sweep to generate a decision lookup table — lets you answer 'given budget B, what should we do?' without committing to a single weighting
Pair a continuous model (ODE + Euler) with a discrete/agent model (cellular automata) for the same dynamics and compare them as mutual validation and to expose effects (locality) one model misses
Calibrate an unknown curve from a few anchor points into a closed form (here: 3 price points → quadratic p(L), then integrate analytically for total cost) when real curves are unavailable

Normalize heterogeneous quantities to a common dimensionless scale (pollutants ÷ total emissions) and reserve an explicit, defaulted-to-1 weighting factor ('badness') so reviewers see the choice and can plug in better values
Cross-validate optimization with two different algorithms (genetic + Nelder-Mead) and report agreement before selecting the faster one

Per-model Strengths & Weaknesses subsections plus a front-loaded assumptions list where each assumption is justified and flagged as 'replaceable with real data' — a template for demonstrating intellectual honesty to judges
A worked 'Example Calculation' that walks a concrete numeric scenario (budget → graph lookup → allocations) to make an abstract optimization usable and convincing
End with a single concrete, quantified recommendation (seed 5–10% in year 1; specific per-source QBTU additions) so the executive reader gets an actionable answer
